speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But to tell you briefly what it was like in the boxcars, it was like a, a, a madhouse. Babies would cry, and, and there was no way to stop them. And food was very difficult to even consume. Even if you had it, it was very difficult to consume. And then as far as, you know, bathroom facilities, there, there were none. I believe-- and I remember, if I'm not mistaken, a bucket of some sort. But I can tell you, I, in all that time, don't remember ever even having gone to the bathroom. It's, it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then finally, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the, the whole period that you were there, it was just kind of wondering where are we going to end up? And then I remember we stopped just outside. It was on a rail. The place looked like a, like a connecting area that will connect to all parts of the world. There were all kinds of railroad tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember exactly, you know, what we were told. But I stood up. I was by the window. I stood up, and I saw one row of boxcars on one side, on our side, and then on the other side, so we were in the middle. And I kind of turned straight ahead, and I, and I was shouting out loud-- to find out who are you, where are you coming from-- to the next transport. And I was told that they're from Amsterdam. So another transport just arrived from Amsterdam. And then the other one-- I don't know where they were from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were told-- as soon as the, the big doors, wide doors opened up-- because the first thing we wanted to know-- where are we? What is this? And you could see and feel and smell this strange place. And the first thing we heard was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Auschwitz, Auschwitz, Birkenau, Birkenau, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So once the, those wide doors slid open, most everybody began to either jump down-- because I don't remember any ramps even being there, either. And if I'm not mistaken, I don't remember how my, my mother even got off. I was a little concerned about it. But they all got out that I remember. But before we could even say anything to each other, there were, there were screams and yells-- men on this side, and women on the other side. Yeah. And so you couldn't-- I don't remember saying goodbye to my mother. But my father and my brother did find ourselves. We, we found ourselves together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were men in striped uniform mingling around. And, and they were sort of cursing at us, yelling and cursing at us, and calling us all kinds of names. What-- didn't you know in 1944 there were-- that this is Auschwitz? Haven't you heard of Auschwitz? And they pointed to the chimneys right away. And that's it. This is the only way you're going to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we wouldn't believe it. Was very-- we didn't want to believe it. You got to be out of your mind. You've got to be mad. What are you telling us, all, all this? But it didn't take too long that we began to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to, to get our thoughts together and, and recognize that this is really and truly more than just a, a labor camp. So the families stayed together when you got out of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were practically-- as we got off, we were practically segregated-- men on one side and women on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were just sort of mingling around until it got a little darker. I remember that. And then my father came over to me and my brother, especially to me, and he said-- because we were told that we're going to go through a selecting process, you know. And my father came over and, and he says, you stand up straight. And you tell the officer that you are 18 years old and that you're a good worker. And that kind of, you know, hit me a little. And I did exactly what he ordered me to, to say and to do. And that happened very quickly. That I know. I was the first. I went through. And just a second or so, and that's it. And I was selected for slave labor. My brother was next. And then my father was after that. And we, we were together for a little bit, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, yes. We were first-- this took place in Birkenau, of course. And then from Birkenau, we were, we were marched across the bridge into Auschwitz. And there we stayed, by the way, in separate barracks, because we got lost in a, in a, in a shuffle in the process of, of being driven from Birkenau from the death section across to Auschwitz, which was supposed to be a work camp. But that, too, was just around the corner from the death camp. And so we stayed there. I, I do know, I remember days-- I would say about three, four days after we arrived, I was wondering where my father was. And so I began to, to look for him. And as I was looking for him, I ran into my brother. And so I asked him to come with me, and let's find my father. And we did. I found him dejected, sitting on the ground. And I tapped him on his shoulder. And he got up on his feet, and, and-- as if wanting to shield me and my brother from the visible flames, you know, that you could see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he began telling me and my brother that we must not stay together. This was not a place for us to be together. And it's no good, he said. And I was kind of wondering why? Why is he telling me all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he began to elaborate. This is a place of death. And, and, and he says, if, if we are together, I will, I will see your suffering. And that's not going to be good for me. And it wouldn't be good for you, either, to see my suffering. And so we have to separate. We've got to be in different places here permanently, he says. Don't look for me. Fight to survive. Don't even think of your mother or your sisters. Think of yourselves and do everything to survive. If and when you do survive, he said, don't forget to tell them what they did to us. And of course, out of all of us, my father, my brother, and my mother and my two sisters, I'm the only one that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz in-- when we arrived-- May, June, July. And then in-- then from there, I was in a Stammlager in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain Stammlager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stammlager is the main camp, which is to be like that I'm not a bypasser. I, I was assigned to, permanently to Auschwitz. But then, then they-- yeah, I was tattooed, of course, yes, in Auschwitz-- A4685. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then Otto Moll-- I, I know the name is not very familiar to many people, but to historians and others know very well who Otto Moll was and what he stood for, who he was in particular. He was in charge of the cremation. He was in charge of the pits, primarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto Moll was a German, yes. Yes. Because he was assigned-- after the annihilation of the Hungarian Jewry, which ended, I would say, around the end of July, beginning of August, he was assigned to become the commandant of a subcamp of Auschwitz called Gleiwitz I. And I was in that subcamp until--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was a Nazi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, of course, well, there's-- he was a Nazi, of course. But he also, he, he was an SS man who had a very powerful role in Auschwitz. Because when the time came to, to destroy the evidence of the existence of the gas chambers at Auschwitz and Birkenau, especially Birkenau, he was assigned to destroy, to level the-- all the gas chambers and crematoria. It was called the Otto Moll Plan, or the Moll Plan-- that's what it was called. And of course, these things I did not know as well as I know now, for a very good reason. Because I began to go back to Auschwitz in '67. And I made very good contact with the officials at the Museum of Auschwitz. And, and, and having gone back, not just periodically for a bus ride or for a visit, but I go back and stay in Auschwitz for a week or 10 days even and do a lot of research. And I got to know the officials at, at the museum. And I'm very well connected along those lines. And I help them put two and two together. And they, in turn, help me to explain my existence in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is there anything else you want to say about Moll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moll was a killer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, I know that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he has-- well, he got caught, of course. I remember when, when all the-- Auschwitz and all the subcamps around Auschwitz were evacuated January the 18th of 1945. Otto Moll was, was in charge of the evacuation, even. He was riding-- and I remember him vividly. He was riding on a tri, tri, tri-car. There was a, a motorcycle with a sidecar, and he sat in the sidecar. And he would always reconnoiter the area where the Russians were. And if the Russians were too close, he'd order us to go back the other way or back this way or go back where you came from. That's unbelievable. And he ordered, of course, that those who could not keep up with the, with the march or the evacuation would be shot. And he made sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were, you were on a, on a death march with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, I was on a death march with Otto Moll. I remember that. But he disappeared. I was on that death march for three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Gross-Rosen. First-- well, of course, it was not all on one shot, you know, from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of Auschwitz transports on foot ended up in Gleiwitz. But then from Gleiwitz, we ended up in Blechhammer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then from Blechhammer to Gross-Rosen. Finally-- and that was a three-week march-- from Gross-Rosen, we were put into open cattle cars and transported to Buchenwald. But Otto Moll was-- he disappeared. And he became in charge of another concentration camp. That was a concentration camp, by the way. And he was caught in 1946 as a prisoner of war. No, I'm sorry-- no, it was in-- he was, he was caught, perhaps, late 1945, put in a prison camp. Then he was tried-- I believe he was tried in Dachau-- convicted, and then executed right there. They hung him. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me-- I ended up in Buchenwald. I arrived in Buchenwald February the 10th. Very shortly after that, I, I contracted typhus. Therefore, I was only maybe one or two days, the most three days, in the upper level of Buchenwald, which was a political prison and whatever. It also had-- it had a sort of a, a-- well, it had some sort of hospital of some sort. And when they found that I had typhus, they transferred me to the little camp, which was the death section of Buchenwald. And there were primarily Jews there. There were some non-Jews there. And they were dying there like flies. It was amazing. I don't think that, that most anyone that was put in there lasted longer than maybe five or 10 days, And I don't know how I was able to survive there until April the 11th, 1945. Because I was not assigned to any work, you know. It was just the death section, waiting, I suppose, just to die. I was a Muselmann already, if you know the term, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe a Muselmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Muselmann-- lethargic, one who has no life in him, one, one who can't even-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, is skin and bone, of course, but it's it. That's the end, with one exception. Even though-- and that's the mystery of, of humanity-- is as long as you have a flicker of life in you, there is a way of making a comeback. And of course, April the 11th was the day when we were liberated. But going back one day before, Buchenwald was in the process of being evacuated. And when the upper level of the camp was being evacuated, there were quite a number of French, Belgian, and Ukrainian, Russian prisoners of war, political prisoners. And they received even Red Cross packages or packages from their home. And when they were, when they were evacuated, a lot of them left their packages behind. And my buddy and I went in there, crossed the line and the fence, even. It was no, you know, no big deal to go through there. And I found cocoa, sugar. And that's all I needed. It brought me back to life within a day or two. You know, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: April the 11th, 1945-- and I remember the day so vividly. It was a bright and sunny day. The guards disappeared. You could see them with their backpacks and their rifles and machine guns [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] running down the hill. Because Buchenwald was on a up hill-- ran down the hill. And I watched. And then, periodically I'd see Allied airplanes come overhead and just even shoot at them. And by 2:00-- well, that was in the morning. And then I see, late in the morning, were-- a lot of those guards apparently left their posts, even, with one exception. The tower was not gone. I did notice all of a sudden prisoners with rifles-- prisoners, striped uniforms. But I was in the upper level of the camp, not on our camp, not on the little camp. And I understand-- that's what I was able to, to, to gather from that-- that they overpowered some of the SS. They went into the guard house and took rifles. And they held a camp. There was no liberation by any Allied Forces. It was held by the political prisoners, first and foremost. By 2:30 in the afternoon, I, I noticed a Jeep with some American soldiers come in. Four o'clock, there were some more coming in. But then we were left alone. I was in that death section, lice-ridden, typhus-ridden, until April the 16th. Yes, April the 16th-- five days in that death section, no one came to even help us. They were afraid of us. We were a plague, you know. No one wanted to come near us. And I know the 16th, I was taken up on the upper level of the camp in a, in, in a brick building which was set up as a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who finally took you up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was carried. I think I was assisted by someone. I was able to use my legs, yes. There were prisoners from the upper level of the camp with white gowns like, you know, nurses of some sort, you know. But by then, we did get already some assistance from the military. And even-- fortunately, I did not do what a lot of my friends did-- ate fast. And there were quite a number who actually ate themselves to death. They couldn't digest that rich food they were cooking right after we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were your liberators?

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberators were the Americans, yes. And I was there, of course. As I said, I was there in April, the whole month of April, then May, June, finally left in July. But almost three and a half to four months, I was recuperating. And to tell you, frankly, all that time that I was recuperating, somehow I sensed and I feared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that this is not liberation for me, that what I'm going to find out is going to be devastating for me. And perhaps it's better that I should have not been liberated. Because I felt that-- I already knew that I had no father. At Buchenwald, I found out from someone who was with my father in Jaworzno, in a subcamp of Auschwitz, in the coal mines. In fact, it was Friedman, the one that he worked for. He told me that your father, oy vey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, what do you mean, oy vey? And then he explained to me later, you know, that he died. He died the last day of Hanukkah, which was December [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1944. He just gave up, apparently. His body was dragged out of the bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother, when the evacuation took place, he either refused to go-- and they took those who did not want to leave out to the woods and shot them. And somehow, I suspected. Because I saw my mother. The following dawn-- you know, we arrived the, the 21st-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19th, 20th-- on the 21st at dawn is when I saw a group of women and children crying and, and, and, and shouting for help. And I spotted a neighbor of ours, the Neuman family-- just her. [NON-ENGLISH] is the way we called her. And then I looked, and I, I spotted my mother, too, and then my two sisters-- a 20-year-old and a 15-year-old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I couldn't believe it. But then I found out what happened, of course, after I went home, that my mother assisted a neighbor of ours, a friend of ours, as a matter of fact, Irving Schwartz-- Irving's sister. Niederman was her name. And one of the little girls was named after my, my grandmother, my mother's mother. And she-- my mother treasured that baby. And she assisted her in, in Auschwitz. And my mother was selected for the gas chamber as a result of that. And of course, my sister, my older sister was so close that she was not about to abandon my mother. So she, she was first selected for slave labor, and my 15-year-old sister also was selected for slave labor. But then she leaped across to be with her mother. And my kid sister followed. And then when that was noticed by the selecting officer, shoved them back into the other line for slave labor. But it didn't take long. Another few minutes, my sister once again leaped across. And my kid sister followed. And the following day at dawn, I saw. That's why I saw them. And this is why I-- see, you cannot survive without thought. So what I did is I blinked it out. It was out. It was not on my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like my father said. Don't think of your mother and your sisters. Survive. So I did what he wanted me to do. I survived. But you want to know the real truth? I often wonder why it was necessary for me to have survived. And when cruelty-- you speak of cruelty in that way, you-- sometimes you will even refer to, you know, to, to your Almighty, to God, and ask Him, why did He have to be so cruel and leave me here? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's the only thing that I cannot level with. [CRYING] I cannot understand that. Why did it have to be me of all of the six? And this is-- to this day, I keep asking myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not a-- it's not something to be thankful about, although I can be told differently by others.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's my conviction, when that happens to a family, it should happen to all if it needs to be happening. And I could never live that off. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] it's not easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 years-- I've lived 50 years after that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you-- anybody even believe that it would be possible to live 50 more years with such awesomeness, with that knowledge [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that He took them, but He wouldn't take me? If you offered me another 50 years, no, thanks. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's not, not an easy thing to deal with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they're asking us to tell those who would like to know what this barbaric event was all about, we can tell, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but there is no way they'll ever understand it. It's useless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is as mysterious as life and death itself, very difficult.
